brief:
JUST ADD ALCOHOL

CONCEPT
A visually-driven, interactive platform that elevates the cocktail experience for both bar owners / restaurants and at-home cocktail enthusiasts. Combines a digital cocktail library with curated non-alcoholic ingredient kits and an interactive bar-side menu.

TARGET AUDIENCE
- Bars and restaurants
- Cocktail enthusiasts at home

PRODUCT OFFERINGS
- Digital Cocktail Library: high-resolution images / looping video, one drink per page with full recipe, educational slides (congeners, shot sizes, fermentation byproducts)
- Curated Ingredient Kits: pre-portioned non-alcoholic components per recipe (mixers, syrups, garnishes, bitters); excludes alcohol
- Interactive Bar Menu: bar checks off available ingredients; customers swipe right to select; QR code to library
- Consumer flash drive / preloaded tablet: digital library playback on home or business TVs

RECIPE LIBRARY (25+ drinks)
Bloody Mary, Caipirinha, Daiquiri, Dark and Stormy, Old Fashioned, Manhattan, Whiskey Sour, French 75, Jungle Bird, Gimlet, Irish Coffee, Tom Collins, Spritz, Mimosa, Martini, Paloma, Sazerac, Pimm's Cup, Pina Colada, Negroni, Mint Julep, Negroni Sbagliato, Mojito, Moscow Mule, Black & White Russian. Each with full ingredient list, instructions, glassware guidance.

BUSINESS BENEFITS
- Stand out with an interactive menu system
- Streamline cocktail creation
- Pre-portioned ingredient kits as additional revenue stream

CONSUMER BENEFITS
- Master mixology with gorgeous visuals
- Professional atmosphere for hosting
- Learn cocktail history and chemistry

REVENUE MODEL
- One-time purchase: flash drive with digital library (consumer)
- Subscription (B2B): monthly / annual for bars
- Sales of curated ingredient kits (recurring)

COMPETITIVE ADVANTAGE
- Visually stunning interface
- Interactive bar-management and customer-selection features
- Curated ingredient kits
- Educational content on cocktail science

NEXT STEPS
- Develop digital library
- Design bar-management and customer-interaction interfaces
- Prototype flash drive and QR code
- Secure partnerships with bar equipment companies and liquor brands

ep_reports.headline: The bar owner buys differentiation; the home host buys competence
ep_reports.content:
TWO BUYERS
This is a two-buyer product - a two-sided platform problem.

BUYER 1: The bar owner
Primary driver: differentiation. The owner is in a crowded category - every bar serves Old Fashioneds. The interactive menu is positioned as the way to be the bar people Instagram, not the bar people stop at. Secondary driver: bartender training (a tablet that shows the recipe reduces training cost on the bartender side).

BUYER 2: The home host
Primary driver: competence performance. The host is throwing a cocktail party and wants to look like they know what they're doing. The flash drive / preloaded tablet is theater - it plays on the kitchen TV while the host "effortlessly" makes the Negroni. The product sells the appearance of expertise.

TRIGGER MOMENTS
- Bar owner: Tuesday afternoon, slow shift, the owner watching the bartender stumble through a French 75 from memory. That's the moment they download the demo.
- Home host: Wednesday before the Saturday dinner party. Panic purchase.

IDENTITY FRAMING
- Bar owner: "the bar that does it right"
- Home host: "the friend who throws good parties"

DANGER
Alcohol-adjacent products carry compliance load that a generic food-CPG brand doesn't. Three-tier system, state-by-state liquor advertising rules, age-gating. The brief must treat this as its own category, not as an extension of a non-regulated food brand - the regulatory weight is fundamentally different.

WALK TO NEXT EP
- Arjun: the regulatory compliance footprint is the biggest non-obvious cost in this category
- Reid: lead with the home-host story for press; the bar-owner story for trade publications

ep_reports.headline: Cocktail-curious home hosts are larger and easier than the bar channel
ep_reports.content:
WHERE IT CAN WORK (top 3)
1. Home hosts in the 28-45 age band who throw 4-12 dinner parties a year. Density: high (US market alone is several million households). Reachable via Instagram, Pinterest, food media.
2. High-end Airbnb hosts who stock the rental and want a curated guest cocktail experience. Density: medium, growing, underserved.
3. Bars and restaurants in tourist markets where menu-as-spectacle drives traffic. Density: high but slow sales cycle.

FOCUS RECOMMENDATION
Home hosts as the beachhead. The home-host purchase decision happens in days; the bar purchase happens in months. The DTC business funds the team while the slower B2B business matures.

ADJACENT BUT DON'T GO YET
- Liquor brands as licensing partners (long, dependent on the brands wanting in)
- Hotels (long procurement)
- Cruise lines (long procurement, regulatory wrinkle of international waters)

OTHER USES (same platform, different category)
- Wine pairing platform (same visual treatment, different content)
- NA cocktail platform (the curated ingredient kits adapt directly to mocktails)
- Coffee-and-tea ceremony platform (international tea ceremonies are visually rich and underserved)

ONE THING WREN WOULD NOT DO
Lead with the bar-platform sale. Same advice as the smoothie platform - the cafe / bar channel is the slowest, the toughest, and the most expensive to serve. Consumer first.

ep_reports.headline: has_legs (signal 6/10). Cocktail content is hot; the platform layer is the trap.
ep_reports.content:
PATTERN: cocktail content + commerce DTC.

WHAT WORKED FOR THIS PATTERN
Death & Co. (cocktail bar + cocktail-content brand, the books print money), Liquor.com (acquired by Dotdash Meredith, profitable), Cocktail Codex (book + brand, scaled to a national bartender training program). The cocktail-content category is one of the best-performing niches in food media.

WHAT FAILED
- Bar-management software with content layers (Square's bar offering, others) generally failed to retain because the content went stale
- Liquor-brand-funded platforms (Diageo's Drinks Mixer, various Pernod-Ricard apps) lost to independent media because consumers don't trust brand-owned content

YOUR POSITION
The content asset is real and sellable. The B2B platform is the part that gets killed - bars don't want another tablet and their existing POS already does the menu work.

LEGS ASSESSMENT: has_legs (signal 6/10)

REASONS FOR
- Cocktail content has proven publisher economics (Liquor.com exit, Death & Co. brand)
- Home host audience is mature and well-mapped
- Non-alcoholic ingredient kits avoid most of the three-tier liquor-distribution complexity
- Founder taste is the moat - recipe curation and photography are the defensible assets

REASONS AGAINST
- Alcohol-adjacent marketing has platform restrictions (Meta, TikTok ad policies are strict)
- Bar B2B sale is hard regardless of product quality
- Direct content competitors (Death & Co., Cocktail Codex, Liquor.com) have decade-old brands

THE ONE THING THAT KILLS IT
Distracting from the consumer content brand with the bar-platform sale, then running out of capital before the consumer brand reaches scale.

ep_reports.headline: Lead with the cocktail library for hosts, the bar platform as a sub-bullet
ep_reports.content:
JUST ADD ALCOHOL LAUNCHES A VISUAL COCKTAIL LIBRARY AND CURATED NON-ALCOHOLIC MIXER KITS FOR HOME HOSTS

[CITY, STATE] - [MONTH DD, YYYY] - Just Add Alcohol, a new visual cocktail library and curated mixer-kit brand for home hosts, today launched on JustAddAlcohol.com. The library covers 25+ classic cocktails - Old Fashioned, Negroni, Whiskey Sour, French 75, Sazerac - each with high-resolution photography, full recipes, glassware guidance, and a brief education on the drink's history and chemistry.

The brand's curated ingredient kits ship the non-alcoholic components for four cocktails per month - bitters, mixers, syrups, garnishes - pre-portioned and recipe-tagged. The host adds the spirit; the kit handles everything else.

"The home host shouldn't have to be a bartender," said [FOUNDER NAME], founder of Just Add Alcohol. "You shouldn't need to buy six bitters and two kinds of bitters and a quarter-bottle of grenadine you use once. We ship the four cocktails you actually want to make this month, in the right amounts."

The monthly subscription is $44, with single-recipe kits available for $14. A free cocktail-library tier is available on the website. A B2B platform for bars and restaurants is in development for 2027.

ABOUT JUST ADD ALCOHOL
Just Add Alcohol is a cocktail-content and ingredient-kit brand built for the home host.

MEDIA CONTACT
[NAME] / [EMAIL] / [PHONE]

ep_reports.headline: Cocktail-aesthetic Instagram + Pinterest, dinner-party-content cadence
ep_reports.content:
PRIMARY POST - INSTAGRAM REEL (Aesthetic + tactical)
A 20-second Negroni build, shot in slow motion, beautifully lit. Voiceover: "Three ingredients. Equal parts. A garnish that looks like you knew what you were doing." CTA: "Free recipe library - link in bio."

FOLLOW-UP CONTENT (45-day calendar)
- Week 1: aesthetic build reels (one cocktail a day for 7 days)
- Week 2: founder POV - why the home host gets it wrong every time
- Week 3: history shorts (the Sazerac is the oldest American cocktail, etc.)
- Week 4: kit unboxing reels from 5 collab creators
- Week 5: dinner-party voiceover series (host's voice over their actual party setup)
- Week 6: glassware deep-dive (the right glass for the right drink)

PINTEREST AND TIKTOK
- Pinterest: every recipe pinned in vertical format with seasonal pairings. Cocktail content performs exceptionally well there.
- TikTok: tactical shorts, history shorts, glassware shorts. The platform's algorithm rewards specific-vertical content.

META / TIKTOK AD POLICY NOTE
Alcohol-adjacent advertising is restricted on Meta and TikTok. The brand can post organic content liberally but paid ads must NOT promote the alcohol. The mixer-kit positioning helps - the kit itself contains no alcohol and can be advertised more freely than a cocktail-content brand could.

WHAT NOT TO POST
- Anything that glamorizes drinking volume
- Anything that targets the under-21 audience (even accidentally)
- Anything that compares spirits brands directly

CONVERSION HOOK
"Free dinner party cocktail menu" - email gate. Subscribers convert to the monthly kit at 10-15% within 60 days.

ep_reports.headline: Cocktail content has publisher economics; lean into them
ep_reports.content:
FRAMEWORKS
- Two-Sided Platform Theory (Rochet, Tirole): a real platform pitfall here - consumer side (home host) and B2B side (bar owner) are fundamentally different products.
- Niche Media Economics (Ben Thompson, Stratechery; Jessica Lessin, The Information): vertical content brands with paid commerce attachments are one of the better-performing media-business models of the 2020s. Liquor.com, Death & Co., and Punch are the cocktail-vertical examples.
- Sensory Marketing (Aradhna Krishna, Michigan Ross): the visual treatment of cocktails is a sensory-marketing intervention. The photography literally drives the sale.

ADJACENT THINKERS
- David Wondrich (cocktail historian, Imbibe! is the canonical book) - any cocktail brand should be in conversation with his work
- Dale DeGroff (King Cocktail, modern American cocktail revival figurehead)
- Maureen Ogle (Ambitious Brew, beer industry history; the alcohol-marketing-regulation history is in her books)

PRIOR LITERATURE
The cocktail-renaissance literature (mostly trade press, Imbibe Magazine and Punch Drink) traces a clear trajectory from the 2007 Death & Co. opening through the modern home-cocktail boom. The home-host segment specifically grew dramatically during COVID and has remained elevated.

ALCOHOL ADVERTISING REGULATION (legal framework)
- TTB (Alcohol and Tobacco Tax and Trade Bureau) regulates alcohol advertising at federal level
- Each state has additional rules (some prohibit certain comparison advertising, age targeting, free-product offers, etc.)
- The mixer-kit positioning (no alcohol in the box) keeps the brand outside most TTB advertising restrictions, but state-level rules on "alcohol-adjacent" content vary

RESEARCH GAP
Little published research on home-host cocktail behavior post-pandemic. A small ethnographic study (50 home hosts, 6 months) would generate both publication and marketing material.

ONE THINKER TO READ FIRST
David Wondrich - the cocktail history is the brand's voice if used correctly.

ep_reports.headline: Alcohol-adjacent compliance is the line; don't ship liquor, ship the mixer
ep_reports.content:
REGULATORY POSITIONING - DO NOT SHIP ALCOHOL
The kit ships non-alcoholic components. The customer adds the spirit. This single design choice keeps Just Add Alcohol outside the three-tier liquor distribution system, outside state-level alcohol shipping prohibitions, and outside TTB licensing as an alcohol producer. KEEP IT THAT WAY.

The moment a kit contains alcohol (even a single bottle of bitters with >0.5% ABV), the regulatory load multiplies 10x: federal TTB registration, state-by-state shipping licenses, age verification at delivery, alcohol-distributor partnerships (the three-tier system mandates them in most states), state-by-state tax filings.

BITTERS - THE EDGE CASE
Most cocktail bitters (Angostura, Peychaud's) are technically alcoholic (35-45% ABV) but are sold as flavorings and exempt from alcohol regulation in most states due to their low-volume use and labeling as "food flavoring." The exemption is state-specific and not universal. Either include bitters and accept state-by-state shipping checks, or exclude bitters from kits and instruct customers to buy them separately.

RECOMMENDATION
Ship mixers, syrups, garnishes, salt rims, dehydrated citrus, and shrubs. Do NOT ship bitters in the kit; provide a link to the recommended bitters for separate purchase. This keeps the kit firmly in food-CPG regulatory territory.

ADVERTISING REGULATION
TTB regulates alcohol advertising. The brand can advertise the mixer kits freely. The brand should NOT use spirit-brand names in paid advertising without spirit-brand approval (cross-brand advertising can trigger TTB review). The brand can use cocktail names freely - cocktails are public-domain recipes.

META / TIKTOK PLATFORM POLICIES
- Meta restricts alcohol advertising to verified advertisers in approved countries; the mixer-kit positioning may exempt the brand from this if approval is sought
- TikTok has stricter restrictions; paid ads need to avoid cocktail-as-content framing
- Organic content is much less restricted; the marketing strategy should lean organic first

FULFILLMENT
Standard food-CPG fulfillment (3PL, co-packer for kit assembly). Cold-chain not required. Shrinkage risk on glass (bitters bottles, garnish jars) - test packaging robustness.

ONE THING TO WATCH
The TTB's interpretation of "alcohol-adjacent advertising" has been case-by-case. If the brand grows large enough to attract attention, expect compliance scrutiny. Budget legal review at $25-50K pre-launch.

ep_reports.report_type: Coaching: Elevator Speech, Strengths, Presenter Prep
ep_reports.headline: Lead with the home host; the bar platform is year-2 optionality
ep_reports.content:
ELEVATOR SPEECH (30 seconds)
"Just Add Alcohol is a cocktail content and mixer-kit brand for home hosts. 25 classic cocktail recipes with photography, history, and glassware. Monthly kits ship the non-alcoholic components for four cocktails; you add the spirit. $44 a month, free library on the website. The hook is the home host who wants to make a great Negroni without buying four bottles of bitters they'll use once. A B2B platform for bars is on the roadmap for 2027."

TOP 10 STRENGTHS TO LEAD WITH
1. Cocktail content has proven publisher economics (Liquor.com exit, Death & Co. brand)
2. The no-alcohol-in-kit design keeps the brand outside three-tier liquor regulation
3. Home host audience is mature, large, post-pandemic-elevated
4. Founder taste in cocktails is the moat (recipes + photography)
5. The mixer-kit design choice (ship no alcohol) is the cleanest possible regulatory posture
6. Cocktail names are public domain - no licensing required
7. Pinterest, Instagram, TikTok all index cocktail content highly in 2026
8. The brand voice (history + chemistry + craft) is differentiable from generic recipe-content brands
9. Premium kit pricing ($44/month) supports good margins
10. Year-2 expansion paths (wine, mocktails, hotel partnerships) are clean

PRESENTER PREP - FACING THE JUDGES

WHAT EACH JUDGE WILL PUSH ON
- Devon Sloane: she'll push on the brand voice and visual identity. Have a sample recipe card and one Reel ready. The craft-and-history voice is your strongest asset.
- Marcus Holt: he'll push on the bar-platform path. Concede that the B2B platform is year-2 optionality, not v1. Show consumer subscription unit economics with clean CAC ($30-50) and LTV ($176 at 4-month adjusted lifetime).
- Astrid Lund: she'll push on alcohol-adjacent compliance. Lead with the no-alcohol-in-kit design choice. That's the compliance answer; don't bury it.

WHEN YOU GET STUCK
Return to the Negroni-without-bitters-shelf moment. Every host has stood in front of the liquor aisle wondering what to buy. The product is that moment, solved.

DO NOT
- Pitch this as a generic recipe-content brand - the alcohol-adjacent regulatory layer is real and differentiating
- Discuss any plan to ship alcohol with the kit (compliance trap)
- Compare the brand to Death & Co. directly - learn from them, don't promise to be them
- Promise the bar B2B platform on a tight timeline

ONE LINE TO REMEMBER
"You add the spirit; we handle everything else."

revisions.before: A visually-driven, interactive platform that elevates the cocktail experience for both bar owners / restaurants and at-home cocktail enthusiasts. Combines a digital cocktail library with curated non-alcoholic ingredient kits and an interactive bar-side menu.
revisions.after: A cocktail-content and curated mixer-kit brand for home hosts, built around a 25-recipe photographed library and a monthly non-alcoholic ingredient kit. A B2B platform for bars and restaurants is on the year-2 roadmap, not part of the launch.
revisions.rationale: The original tried to launch a consumer brand and a B2B platform at once - the canonical two-sided-platform trap. The revised version commits to consumer-first and explicitly defers the bar platform. This decision pays for itself across every downstream module.

judge_outputs.stage_critique: The cocktail-content concept is clear and the audience is well-imagined. The clarity gap is the classic two-sided platform one - the brief tries to be both a consumer brand and a B2B platform. Pick the consumer brand for v1.

The brief reads like two product pitches stapled together. An investor reads it and asks what they're funding. Pick the cocktail-content-and-kit business; treat the bar platform as future optionality.

The regulatory framing is unclear because the brief doesn't commit to whether bitters are in the kit. That single line item determines whether you ship under food-CPG rules or trigger alcohol-distribution rules. The brief should specify.

The consumer-brand viability is strong. The home-host audience is real, post-pandemic-elevated, and reachable through visual social. Founder taste in cocktails is the moat. The viability is contingent on the photography and recipe development quality.
The unit economics work at $44/month with conservative churn. Kit-DTC churn is the kill variable - plan for 50%+ first-90-day churn and design retention around the content side, not the kit alone.

Viability is real if the alcohol-adjacent advertising restrictions are navigated correctly. The brand can grow organically but paid acquisition is harder than for a typical food-CPG brand. Budget more for content production and creator partnerships, less for paid acquisition.

The brand-risk is treating cocktail content as a generic recipe platform with a thin alcohol overlay. Cocktail audiences expect craft, history, and chemistry as the brand voice. The brand has to commit to that voice fully - half-measures read as inauthentic to the audience that buys this category.

The capital risk is launching two consumer brands (smoothies and cocktails) from one cap table. Each needs its own marketing and content budget; combined they exceed what a single seed raise can support. Sequence them.

The compliance risk is contained as long as the kit ships no alcohol. The brief should print that as a hard rule. The moment a marketing intern decides bitters "don't count," the brand is in TTB territory and the recovery cost is severe.

The craft-and-history narrative is the strongest available. Cocktails carry an inherent storytelling weight that smoothies don't. Lean into the history shorts as a content engine - they're inexpensive to make and dense with brand voice.
The narrative needs to commit to a buyer. The home-host narrative is clean. The bar-owner narrative is different and currently dilutes the brand voice. Pick one for the launch.

The educational-content angle (congeners, fermentation byproducts) is interesting but academic for the home-host audience. Tune it - the home host wants the why-this-glass and the why-this-garnish, not the chemistry lecture. Save chemistry for a deeper-cut content tier.
No evidence on consumer interest yet. Run a 50-host pre-launch beta. Photograph their setups using your recipes. The evidence becomes the marketing.
The unit-economics evidence is missing. Run a 100-subscriber soft launch before scaling marketing. Real numbers on conversion, retention, and AOV will make or break the Series A pitch in year 2.

The regulatory-evidence layer needs to be in writing - a one-page memo from a beverage-alcohol attorney confirming the kit-with-no-alcohol design exempts the brand from TTB licensing. That memo is the brand's compliance shield.

The cultural moment for home cocktails is strong. The post-pandemic home-host habit stuck, and the craft-cocktail-as-aesthetic-content moment on Instagram and Pinterest is still up-trending. The brand fits the moment cleanly.

The cultural read is good. The investor cultural-fit question is whether the alcohol-adjacent advertising restrictions deter generalist VCs. Position the brand as content-and-commerce rather than as a liquor-adjacent product to widen the investor pool.

The cultural fit is strong on cocktail content but the brand sits inside the broader cultural conversation about alcohol moderation (sober-curious, NA cocktail growth). The brand should be in conversation with that trend - perhaps a parallel mocktail line - rather than ignore it.

The competence-performance psychology of the home host is read precisely. The host buys the appearance of expertise - the photography sells that, the recipe carries through, the curated kit makes it possible. The psychology coheres.

The buyer-psychology is solid on the consumer side. The bar-owner psychology, if you keep that audience in v1, is different - owners buy on differentiation and labor cost, not aesthetic performance. Drop the bar-owner audience and the psychology coheres.

The host's psychological need for risk-reduction (don't embarrass yourself at the party) is the under-leveraged angle. The pre-portioned kit removes the risk of buying the wrong bitters and ruining the drink. Lean into the risk-reduction framing in marketing - it's a more honest read of the buy moment than "learn mixology."

The brand-compliance layer for alcohol-adjacent content is the part most consumer-brand founders miss. Every social post, every email, every influencer collab needs review. Build the operational discipline now - it's cheaper than retrofitting it post-FTC inquiry.

Compliance is manageable as long as the design discipline holds (no alcohol in kit, no spirit-brand advertising without permission). The cost of a compliance attorney on retainer is much smaller than the cost of one TTB inquiry.

The compliance footprint covers TTB advertising, state-by-state alcohol-adjacent rules, FTC influencer disclosure, Meta and TikTok platform policies, and standard food-CPG labeling. Each is achievable but the brief currently treats them as a single line. They're not.
